**The Lighthouse, St. Agnes Point**  
**October 1st, 1893**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a quill that trembles not from the cold, but from the weight of words I have kept locked away for too many years. You will not be surprised to learn that I have not heard from you since the day you left, and I dares not hope that you will read this, for I fear the sea may claim me before the ink dries.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs that guard this lighthouse. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, and though the light cuts through the storm, I cannot shake the feeling that it is not meant for the world beyond these walls. The storm is not merely a weather event, my dear—this night is a reckoning.

I have not spoken your name in five years, nor have I written it. I have kept your letters folded in the drawer beneath my bed, and I have burned your last missive in the hearth, for I could not bear the sight of your ink upon the page. You wrote of London, of the theatre, of the life you wished for, and I replied with the language of a man who had already buried the son he once was. I was not the father you needed, and I have never been the man you

**[Lighthouse, Cape Varn, December 19th, 1902]**

**To: Eleanor "Ellie" Varn**  
**From: Captain James Varn**  
**Lighthouse Keeper, Cape Varn**

---

**Cape Varn Lighthouse**  
**Cape Varn, Nova Scotia**  
**December 19, 1902**

**[Envelope sealed with wax bearing the crest of the Varn family]**

---

**To My Dearest Ellie,**

If you're reading this, it means I didn't make it. Or perhaps you've already found this letter hidden among the old logs and salt-stained papers in the lighthouse archives. Either way, I hope this reaches you in time.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since I last saw you, and five years since I stopped writing to you. I've tried, more than once, to reach out—through letters, through the postman, through the occasional visit from the local magistrate—but every time, the response was the same. You never answered. You never came. And so, I stopped.

But tonight... tonight is different.

The storm is coming. The sky is black as ink, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The waves are rising, crashing against the cliffs with a fury that feels almost human. I've been here for over twenty years, and I've seen my share of storms,

*Grabs a tattered notebook from the shelf, the pages yellowed with age and salt. His fingers tremble as he uncorks a bottle of ink and begins to write, the flickering lamp casting long shadows across the creaking wooden desk. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, rattling the shutters. He pauses, then exhales a shuddering breath, and begins to write...*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

Five years. Five long, bitter years since the last time your voice stirred the air in this lighthouse. I have not spoken your name in the manner it was meant to be spoken. Not with warmth, nor with love—only with the cold, hard weight of silence that has settled like the sea on my bones.

The storm is coming, child. Not just the wind, nor the rain, but something deeper. A reckoning. I can feel it in my marrow, in the creak of the timbers, in the way the sea roars like it has something to say. And I... I fear I may not see the dawn.

You were not the first to leave me, nor shall you be the last. But you were the first to take your light with you. I had hoped, in time, that the fire would burn itself out in your absence. That the sea would swallow the sound of your footsteps. But it has not. It has only grown louder.

I have kept the lamp burning these

**April 12, 1895**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From Your Father,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. I write to you now not with the hope of hearing from you, but with the intention of saying what I have long held in my heart—words that have grown heavier with each passing year.

It is now the eve of the storm, and the wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone of this lighthouse. The sea, once so calm and kind, now rages with a fury that seems almost human. I have not slept in days, and I do not know if I will be able to sleep tonight. The lamps are lit, as they must be, and I have prepared the emergency supplies. But I have also prepared my thoughts.

Eleanor, I have not spoken to you in five years. Five years is a long time to keep a silence that grows louder with each day. I have tried, I truly have. I wrote to you once, in the spring of 1890, and I received no reply. I wrote again in the autumn of 1891, and again in 1893. Each time, I was met with silence. I understand now that you did not wish to hear from me. Perhaps you believed I had not changed. Perhaps you believed I had not learned. Or perhaps, like me, you

**The Keepers’ Cottage,  
Wick Point,  
October 12th, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I find myself in a state of quiet desolation this evening, as the wind howls like a banshee through the rocks and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs themselves. The lamps are lit, the fires are stoked, and the bell has been rung thrice—once for the tide, once for the wind, once for the soul that lingers in the cold air.

It is a cruel irony, I fear, that the very night which calls for the most vigilance is the one which brings me to the writing of this letter. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if the sea will carry this missive to your door or if it will be dashed upon the rocks like the waves that have long since worn down the very stones of this cliff.

You were but a child when you last stood in the light, your small hand clasping mine as you gazed upon the sea with wide, curious eyes. I remember the way the lantern’s glow lit your face, and how you whispered, “It’s like the stars have fallen into the sea.” I told you it was the sea’s own fire, and that it would always be there to guide the ships, just as it would guide you through the darkest of nights.

But the sea is

My dearest Clara,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may find you in tears. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have been trying, in my own way, to make sense of the silence between us. You were so young when you left, and I didn't understand then why you would choose to walk away from your father. But now, as I sit here in this lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, I find myself wondering if I ever truly understood you at all.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones. The sea is restless, the sky is dark as ink, and the waves are rising with a fury that makes my hands tremble. I've spent my life watching the horizon, keeping watch for ships, for danger, for the unknown. But tonight, I'm not sure I can keep watch for much longer. The light is flickering, and I fear it may go out before dawn.

I’ve always been the one to stay, Clara. I’ve always been the one to keep the lighthouse running, to make sure no one is lost to the sea. But I’ve never been the one to stay with you. I told myself it was for your own good, that you needed to grow up, that you needed to see the world. But I never asked if you needed me.

I remember the day you left. You were seventeen, just like you are now

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea roiling with fury. The lighthouse stands tall, but I fear the storm may be more than it can bear. I've lit the beacon, as I always do, though tonight it feels like a futile act against the chaos beyond the stone walls.

Five years have passed since you left, and I've not spoken to you. Not once. I've kept your name in my heart, but not in my letters. I've kept your memory in the lantern's glow, but not in my voice. I've watched the waves crash against the cliffs, and I've wondered if they carry your voice, if they whisper your name back to me in the dark.

I know you blame me. You think I was too harsh, too strict. That I locked you out of my life, that I chose duty over family. But I never meant to lose you. I thought I was protecting you, keeping you safe from the world that had already taken too much. You were young, and I was afraid of what the world would do to you. I thought the sea was safer than the streets.

But I see now that I was wrong. You were not meant to be locked away, not in my house, not in my heart. You needed to find your own way, to live your own life. And I was too afraid to let you go.

**My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I suspect it may not. I’ve been meaning to write for some time now, but the sea has been restless, and so have I. You see, I’ve been the lighthouse keeper at Skerry Point for over a decade now, and I’ve grown quite accustomed to the solitude. But lately, I’ve felt a strange restlessness, as though the sea itself is calling me to do something I’ve long avoided.

It’s been five years since we last spoke, Clara. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of your absence in my life. I understand that. I’ve made my share of mistakes, and I’ve been the one to blame for most of them. I should have reached out sooner, but I was afraid. Afraid of what you might say, of what you might think of me. And so, I let time pass, and you drifted away, as I did.

But now, as the storm is upon us, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. The winds are rising, the waves are growing fiercer, and I’ve been told that the storm is unlike any we’ve seen in years. The coast guard has warned us, and I’ve been preparing for it. I’ve made sure the lighthouse is in order, the lights are working, and the fuel is stocked. But I’ve also been thinking about the light we both lost—our

**To My Lily,**

I sit here by the fire, the wind howlin’ like a banshee, and I can’t help but think o’ ye. Five years, Lily. Five years since ye left, and still I can’t seem to let go o’ the sight o’ that door, shut tight and cold, like the world itself had turned its back on me.

I ain’t wrote nothin’ to ye in all that time, not a word. I couldn’t, not when I knew ye were out there, somewhere, with that fancy city life and all its lies. But now, as the storm’s comin’ in like the devil’s own hand, I feel like I’ve got to say somethin’. Not for me, not really. But for the boy that’s been keepin’ the light these past five years, and for the man I once was, who used to know how to love.

You remember the way the sea used to call me, Lily. How the waves would come in like a mother’s lullaby, and I’d feel like I was part o’ it, all the way down to my bones. I used to tell ye that the light was more than just a lamp, that it was a promise. That I’d keep it burnin’ for you, for the ones I loved, and for the days when the world was too dark to see.

But I guess I let the days get too dark

**[Sealed with a wax impression of a broken compass]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the quill trembling in my hand, for I fear the storm that rages now beyond these stone walls shall soon be upon us. The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea, that old and cruel companion, roars with fury as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not the strength to keep the light burning for long. I am but a poor, old man, and the sea is not so kind as your father.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left me. I have not seen you, nor heard from you, nor even received a single letter. You were always a spirited child, but I fear you grew into a woman who would not tolerate the smallest inconvenience. I was not a man of riches, nor of great importance. I was a lighthouse keeper, a humble servant of the sea, and yet you deemed me unworthy of your regard.

I have tried to forgive you, but the sting of your departure remains. You left without a word, as if I had been a stranger to you. I would have given you everything—your education, your future, your place in society—had you but asked. But you did not. You thought yourself too grand for such a man as I. You thought the world would bend to your will, and that you could command the very air to

**April 12, 1878**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast from the depths of the sea, and the sky dark as a tomb. The sea is restless, and the lighthouse stands defiant, though I fear it may not hold for much longer. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn.

It is strange, this silence. I have been a lighthouse keeper for twenty years now, and in all that time, I have never been more alone than I am now. The light has been my only companion, and now, even it seems to falter in the face of this storm.

I have always believed that duty is the highest calling, and I have served with unwavering loyalty to the Crown and to the people of this coast. I have watched countless ships come and go, and I have seen the sea take many lives. But never have I felt so powerless as I do now, as I watch the storm gather, knowing that I may not be able to save even myself.

You were not the first to leave me, but you were the last. I have thought of you often, Eliza. I have imagined your face in the light of the beacon, and I have wondered if you would have been proud of me, or if you would have called me a fool. I have

> *My Dearest Clara,*

> Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and yet it feels as though the days have only stretched into an endless night. The sea, once a familiar companion, now roars with a fury that echoes the storm that gathers beyond the cliffs. Tonight, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sky is a canvas of black and silver, as though the heavens themselves weep for what has been lost. I write this with trembling hands, for I fear that this may be the last night I shall ever see, and I must not leave you with silence.

> I have not spoken to you in years, and I know that my silence has been a dagger in your heart. I have no excuse for it, not even the weight of the sea or the burden of duty. You were never a burden to me, Clara. You were my light — the only thing that ever truly warmed the cold, lonely nights in this tower. I have not seen you in years, and I have not written, and I have not called, and I have not loved you as I should have. I have let the silence grow between us like ivy, and I have let it choke the words I once had for you.

> I know that you were hurt by my absence, and I know that you may not even read this. I do not know if you will ever forgive me, and I do not know if

**The Lighthouse**  
**Havendean Cliff**  
**12th November, 1889**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the wind howling like the very devils that once roamed these moors, and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very cliffs themselves. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse before the night is through.

It has been five years since your last letter, and five years since I last heard your voice. I have kept your address, and I have written, but the post office returned them all—undelivered, or with the seal broken. I suppose they were never meant for you. I wonder if you still live at 34 Highgate Street, or if you have moved on to some grander place, where the sea and the wind are but distant memories.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I was alone in the tower, the light burning steady, when the carriage came. You stood at the door, your bonnet still damp from the rain, and said, “I cannot stay, Father.” I did not argue. I did not beg. I simply said, “Be safe, Eliza.” And you went, without looking back.

I have not been the same since. The lighthouse is still here, the light still burns, but the man who tends it is not the same. I have grown old

**Lighthouse Keeper's Log**  
**April 14, 1897**  
**North Sea Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  

**To: Eleanor "Ellie" Whitaker**  
**From: Captain James Whitaker**  
**Date: April 14, 1897**  

**[Postscript: Not to be sent. To be burned after reading.]**

---

**North Sea Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  
**April 14, 1897**

**To My Daughter, Ellie:**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these walls. The sea is restless, the skies are black, and the lighthouse stands as a solitary sentinel against the storm. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, I find myself writing to you as though the silence between us has never existed.

You were ten when I last saw you, standing at the edge of the garden, your small hand clutching a dandelion, your eyes full of questions and a quiet defiance. You were everything I had hoped for, and yet, I let you go. I let you leave, and I let the silence grow between us. I told myself it was for your own good. I told myself that the world was too cruel, that the world needed you to be somewhere else, somewhere safe. But I was wrong.

I have spent

*Grabs a tattered piece of parchment from the desk, his hands steady despite the tremble in his voice. The flickering lantern light casts long shadows across the room. He pours a dram of whiskey, takes a slow sip, and begins to write with the worn quill in his hand.*

---

**To My Dearest Elara,**

*The wind howls like a banshee in the distance, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I’ve lit the lantern, as I always do, though tonight the light feels heavier, as though it carries the weight of all I’ve left unsaid. The storm is coming—*a *storm like none I’ve seen in my years here. And I fear it may take more than the lighthouse.*

Five years, Elara. Five long, bitter years since the last time your voice stirred the silence of this keep. I’ve heard the stories, of course. The tales of a man lost to drink, a father who turned his back on his daughter. I’ve heard the whispers of the sea, how it swallows men who forget their duty. But I’ve not forgotten, not truly. Not ever.

I’ve watched the waves crash against these rocks, night after night, and I’ve thought of you. Of your laugh, the way you’d run through the fog with your bonnet askew, and your mother’s voice calling you back. I’ve watched the

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you from the lighthouse, where the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury that makes even the most seasoned sailors tremble. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that will test the limits of both man and nature. I fear I may not see dawn.

It has been five years since we last spoke, and I have spent those years in silence, not out of malice, but because I did not know how to say the words that would have broken my heart. You were a child when we parted, and I, a man who had long since forgotten how to be a father. I thought I could protect you by keeping you at a distance. I was wrong.

I have always loved you, Eliza. More than life itself. But I was a fool to believe that love alone could shield you from the world’s cruelty. I was a fool to think that I could keep you safe from the pain of loss, from the weight of expectations, from the truth that I was not the man you needed.

I know you must have heard the rumors, the whispers that I was too harsh, too distant, too afraid to love. I was. I was afraid. Afraid of failing you, of being a disappointment, of not being the father you deserved. I was afraid of what I might lose if I reached for you.

But now, as I sit here in the storm, with

**The Lighthouse, Skerry Point**  
**November 17th, 1887**  

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee upon the cliffs and the sea roaring as if it would devour the very stones of this place. The lantern burns low, and the light’s flame flickers like a dying soul. I fear the storm may yet claim me before the dawn, and so I must write—though I know not if the sea will carry my words to you, or if they will be lost to the tempest.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years since I last set foot in the drawing room. The house, once warm and filled with the scent of lavender and hearthfire, now stands silent and cold. The sea, ever restless, has taken the sound of your laughter, and the wind has carried away the scent of your gloves and the scent of your tears.

I’ve not spoken to you in years, and yet I’ve not forgotten. I’ve not forgotten the night you stood at the door, your bonnet askew, your eyes red and your hands trembling with the cold. You told me you’d come to see me, and then you turned away. I said nothing, for I feared my voice would break. I feared the sea would drown me before I could speak the words that would have brought you back.

You were angry, I believe

My Dearest Clara,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know you may not be. I’ve been meaning to write for years, but words have always felt like the wrong thing to say. They are too fragile, too easily misunderstood. But tonight, as the storm howls outside and the wind rattles the windows like it’s trying to get in, I feel the need to speak. Not just to you, but to myself.

It has been five years since you left. Five years since you said you couldn’t stay. I remember the look in your eyes that night—so much sadness, so much disappointment. I didn’t understand it then, and I still don’t. But I do know that you were hurting, and I was too stubborn to see it. I was too proud to admit that I’d failed you.

You were only sixteen when you left, Clara. A girl with a fire in her chest and a heart full of dreams. I thought I was protecting you, but I was just holding you back. You wanted to be a writer, to explore the world, to live your life. And I, a lighthouse keeper with more patience than sense, told you you needed to stay. That you needed to be safe. That you needed to be like me.

But you were never like me. You were always more like your mother—bold, passionate, full of questions. And I was too afraid to let you go. I was too afraid

My Dearest Clara,

I sit by the light, the beacon that once guided ships safely to shore, now flickering with the weight of my silence. The wind howls outside, a sound that echoes the sorrow I've carried in my heart for years. Tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must write, for I have something to say before the waves take me.

Five years have passed since I last saw you. Five years of letters that went unanswered, of words left unspoken. I have been your father, your keeper, your guardian, and yet you have turned your back on me. I do not know if it was the pain of your mother's death, or the bitterness of your own heart, or perhaps the weight of your own dreams that made you choose to walk away. But I have not forgotten you, Clara. You are always in my mind, in my heart, in the light I keep burning for the ships that pass.

I have always believed in the light. It is not just a beacon for sailors—it is a promise, a hope, a reminder that even in the darkest nights, there is still a way forward. I have watched the sea for so many years, and I have learned that storms are not the end. They are the tests of endurance, the trials that prove the strength of those who face them. And I have always believed that you, too, would face your storms with courage.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay I’ve been more concerned with the state of my own health lately. It’s been five years since we last spoke, and I have to admit, I’ve been meaning to write. But the sea has been restless, and my hands have been too shaky to hold a pen for more than a few minutes at a time.

I’ve always been a man of routine, Eliza. The lighthouse, the light, the watchful eye over the horizon. But lately, the routine has been faltering. The storm is coming—terrible, monstrous, and I fear it may not be the sea we should be worried about. The winds are already howling like the wails of the damned, and the waves are rising higher than I’ve seen in decades. I’ve been keeping the light burning all night, just in case, and I’ve made sure the beacon is set to automatic. But I’m not sure I’ll be here to see the morning.

I’ve been thinking a lot about you lately, more than I should. It’s not that I’ve forgotten you, Eliza. It’s that I’ve been trying to. You were always so full of life, so full of questions, and I wasn’t always the best at answering them. I was a man of the sea, not of the heart, and I suppose I let my duty come before my family.

**Letter from the Lighthouse, April 15th, 1893**

*To My Dear Eliza,*

I sit here by the fire, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls, and I feel the weight of the sea on my bones. The lantern’s light flickers, but it’s steady, as it always has been. I’ve kept it burning for twenty-three years now, and I reckon it’s the only thing that’s ever been steady in this life of mine.

I’ve been meaning to write you, Eliza. I’ve meant to write you for five years, since the day you walked out that door with that young man of yours and left me with the lighthouse and the silence. I’ve been trying to find the words, but they always seem to slip away like the tide on a moonless night. I don’t know what I’d say to you, not after all these years. Not after the way you left.

But tonight’s different. Tonight, the sea’s getting restless. I’ve seen it coming for days—dark clouds rolling in like a curse, the wind howlin’ like it’s trying to tear the sky down. The boys down at the village said it’s one of the worst storms they’ve seen in years. I don’t know if I’ll make it through the night, Eliza. I don’t know if I’ll wake up tomorrow morning, or if the sea’ll take

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I have been meaning to write for some time now, but my hands have been too steady, my mind too troubled. You see, I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that time may soon be at an end.

The weather is turning, as it often does in this place. The sea, which has always been my companion, has grown restless. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves have grown more savage than ever. I have no doubt that tonight will be the worst storm this coast has seen in many a year. The lighthouse, though strong, is not impervious to the fury of nature, and I fear that I may not see the dawn.

I have always believed that the sea is a force of both creation and destruction, and I have spent my life ensuring that its power is tempered. It is a duty I have taken upon myself with solemn pride, and one I have performed without complaint. But I have never been able to say the same of my duty to you.

You were young when you left, and I thought it for the best. You had your own life to live, your own ambitions. I did not understand then, as I do not understand now, that you were not merely running away. You were running from something, or perhaps from someone. I have never dared to ask, for I feared the answer

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1876**

**To the Captain of the *Dover Castle*,**

**I have not written to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the sea howls and the wind screams like the devil himself, I find myself compelled to put pen to paper.**

I am writing from the lighthouse, which stands tall and defiant against the storm. The light has been lit, as it has been for thirty years, and I have done my duty, as I have done my duty since the day I took this post. But tonight—tonight is different. The sea is restless, the sky is black, and I fear that the light may not be enough to guide any vessel through this tempest. I have seen storms before, but never one like this. The waves are rising, and I am not sure I will be able to hold the lamp steady. I am not sure I will be able to hold myself steady.

You were always the child who looked to the sea with wonder, Eleanor. You once told me that you believed the light from the lighthouse was the eyes of the gods watching over the world. I used to smile at that, but now, as I watch the storm, I wonder if there is not some truth in it. Perhaps the gods do watch. Perhaps they are watching now.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know that I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. The waves crash against the cliffs with a violence that feels almost personal. I have stood here for over thirty years, guiding ships through the darkness, but tonight, I fear I may not be able to guide myself.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unopened, of nights spent staring at the horizon, wondering if you had ever looked back. I have tried to write to you, but every word feels like a blade. I fear that if I speak, I will only wound you again.

I know you left because of what I said. I know you believed I had no right to ask you to stay. But Eleanor, I never meant to hurt you. I thought I was protecting you from a world that would never understand you. A world that would never see the light in your eyes, the fire in your soul.

You were my greatest joy, and yet I let fear and pride keep me from reaching for you. I thought I could guard you from the storms of life, but I have only made you run from me. And now, as I stand on the edge of the cliff, the storm rising like a dark tide, I wonder if I have done more harm than good.

You were not meant to be alone. You were not meant to carry the weight of a world that has never seen

**The Lighthouse**  
**St. Agnes Head**  
**The Isle of Wight**  
**April 1st, 1893**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and silver. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and the light cuts through the gloom like a blade. I pray it will guide some vessel safely through the maelstrom.

I have not heard from you in five years, Eleanor. I have not written, and I have not sent. I have not even dared to send a letter to your address in London, for I feared the words might not be read, or worse, that they might be returned with a note that would cut deeper than the sea.

I have not forgotten you, my child. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the way you stood at the door, your bonnet tilted, your eyes like two cold stones. You said you were going to make something of yourself, that you could not be bound by the tides and the stones and the quiet of this place. You said you would not be like me—stuck in a world of salt and solitude.

But I have always been a man of the sea, Eleanor. I

**[Envelope]**

**[Addressed to: Margaret E. Thompson]**  
**[Postmarked: April 3, 1892]**  
**[Lighthouse: Point Arguel, Nova Scotia]**  
**[Postage: 3c]**  

---

**[Inside the letter]**

**[Written in a shaky, ink-stained hand, with some smudges and uneven lines, as if written in the dark with a flickering lamp]**

**To My Dearest Margaret,**

**[A pause, a scratch out, then a new line]**

**To My Dearest Daughter,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and the storm that has been gathering for days is upon us. I fear I may not see the dawn.

I’ve been waiting for this letter to come to me — or rather, for *you* to come to me. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and the silence between us has grown as vast as the Atlantic. I’ve tried to reach you, to write, to call, but the letters always seem to vanish, or arrive too late. I’ve even tried sending them through the post office in Halifax, but the last one was returned with a note: *No address, no recipient.* It was signed by a woman with the initials *M.E.T.* — which I suppose is

*Seals the letter with a trembling hand, the ink smudged from the salt and the tears that have long since dried upon his weathered face. The wind howls like a banshee through the stone walls of the lighthouse, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very cliffs themselves. He lights a candle, its flickering flame casting long shadows across the parchment, and begins to write.*

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you are reading these words, then I have not yet been claimed by the sea—or by the silence that has grown between us. Five years. Five long, bitter years. Time has been a cruel thing, has it not? It does not forgive, nor does it forget. It simply lingers, like the salt in my bones, or the weight of the lantern in my hands.

I have kept this lighthouse for many years now, you know. The years have worn me thin, but the light must burn. It is the only thing that keeps the dark at bay. And I—ah, I have been the only thing keeping the dark at bay. For you, perhaps. I do not know. I have not spoken your name in so long, not since the night you left. I have not spoken at all, not since that night.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones. The sky is a bruised violet, the wind a beast with a voice of iron. The

**April 12, 1893**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, my heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken and the fear of what may come. The sea, as ever, is restless tonight, and the wind howls like the wails of the damned. I have not seen the dawn in five years, and I fear I may not see the sun again.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I have not written, either. I have been a lighthouse keeper, a husband, a father — but I have not been a father to you. I have not been a man. I have been a ghost, lingering in the shadows of this lonely rock, watching the world pass me by.

You were only twelve when you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday — the tears in your eyes, the way you clung to your mother's hand, and the way you whispered, *“I’ll come back, Papa.”* I told you I would wait. I told you I would always be here. But I did not understand the price of that promise. I did not understand that the world would not wait for me, and that you would not return.

I have tried, Eleanor. I have tried to be the man you needed. I have tried to be the father you deserved. But I

**The Lighthouse, East Cliff, Devon**  
**October 3rd, 1884**  

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

It has been five years since you left this place, and five years since I have heard aught of you save the wind howling through the stones and the sea gnawing at the cliffs. I suppose it is not uncommon for a man to be alone in such a place, though I have never thought myself a man of much comfort. But tonight—*tonight*—the wind is not like the others. It is a beast, a fiend, and I fear the sea may have the last word in this tale.

I have not written to you in years, nor have you written to me. I daresay you think me a fool, or worse, a brute. Perhaps I have been both. But I have kept the lamp burning for you, Eliza, and the light has been seen from the farthest reaches of the coast. I have kept the beacon lit, as I have done for thirty years, and I have kept it lit for you, though you have not been here to see it.

The storm comes upon us with a fury that is unnatural. The waves are like the wrath of the gods, and the wind howls like a banshee. I have seen the sea rage in this place before, but never with such a maddening, unrelenting force. I have

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I suspect it may find you in a state of disbelief. It has been five years since I last saw you, and I have been trying, in vain, to reach out. I know you've been hurt, and I understand why. You were only ten when I left, and I was a fool. I thought I could protect you by leaving, but I only left you alone.

Tonight, the storm is coming. It's the kind that rattles the windows and makes the sea howl like a wounded beast. I've spent my life keeping this lighthouse lit, guiding ships through the darkest nights, but I fear I may not make it through this one. The wind is howling like it's trying to tear the roof off, and the waves are crashing against the cliffs with such force, I can barely hear myself think.

I've spent these years thinking about you, Eleanor. I've thought about the way you used to run up to me with your little hands clasped around my fingers, the way you'd ask me if the sea was going to eat the lighthouse. I used to tell you it was just a big, friendly wave, but I know now that I was wrong. I was trying to make you feel safe, but I was just running away from my own fears.

I've been so selfish, Eleanor. I didn't realize how much you needed me until I was gone. I

My Dearest Clara,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not reach you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast at my door, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. I have been here all my life, watching the waves, the stars, the endless horizon, and now, with the storm bearing down, I feel the weight of the years between us pressing heavily on my chest.

You have not written, nor have you come to visit, and I have not spoken to you in five years. I have tried to forget, to move on, to let the silence between us grow into something that could not be undone. But the storm is coming, and I do not know if I will survive it. I have always been the one to stand watch, to guard this place, to keep the light burning. But tonight, I fear I may be the one who is lost to the sea.

I have thought of you often, Clara. Not in anger, but in sorrow. I have watched the waves crash against the cliffs, wondering if you ever look out at the sea from your own window, wondering if you ever think of the life I chose, the life that took me away from you. I have no regrets, not for the choices I made, but for the ones that cost you your father.

You were always my greatest joy, and I would have given anything to have you here with me, to share

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it's more likely to find you in a state of confusion or perhaps even irritation. I imagine you’ve heard from your mother by now, and I daresay she’s told you I’ve been “unreachable” or “unavailable.” I suppose that’s the polite way of saying I’ve been a terrible father.

It’s been five years, Eleanor. Five years since the last time we spoke. Five years since I last saw your face, heard your voice, or held you close. Time has a way of moving on without us, doesn’t it? And I’ve been left behind, watching it all go by.

I’m writing to you now from the lighthouse, which I’ve called home for the past twenty years. The sea is restless tonight, and the storm is coming. I’ve been keeping watch, as I always have, and I’ve been thinking a lot about you. I don’t know if it’s the storm or the loneliness, but I’ve been feeling a bit unmoored lately. Perhaps it’s both.

You were always the clever one, Eleanor. I remember how you used to sit by the window, sketching the sea and the lighthouse, as if you were trying to capture something more than just the view. You had a mind like a well-ordered library, and a heart that was always open. I used to admire that.

**To My Little Rose,**

I've been sitting here by the light, the wind howling like a beast in the night, and I thought 'twas time I wrote to ye. It's been five years, Rose, five years since ye left this place. Five years since ye walked out that door with that sharp tongue of yers and that stubborn heart of yours, and left me here with the sea and the stars and the silence.

I've been keepin' this light burnin', Rose. Every night, every storm, every time the sky turned black and the waves roared like hell itself, I've kept that light burnin'. I've kept it for ye, even when I didn't know why. I thought maybe ye'd come back one day, like the tide come back to the shore. But ye didn't. And I didn't call out. Not once.

The wind's gettin' worse. I can hear it now, like it's tryin' to tear the roof off. I've been keepin' the fire goin', but I'm tired, Rose. Tired of the sea, tired of the silence, tired of waitin' for a word from ye. I've been thinkin' a lot lately, 'bout the days when ye used to come here, when ye'd sit on the porch and listen to the waves, and talk to me 'bout the stars and the moon. I'd tell ye stories,

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles more from the weight of my thoughts than from the cold. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside this window, and the sea, that old adversary of mine, roars with a fury I have never known. I have kept this lighthouse for thirty years, and never before have I feared for my life. But tonight, I fear not for my life alone, but for the fate of my soul, and for the daughter who once called me “father.”

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years since I last laid eyes upon you. I have not seen you since that day you stormed into my study, your eyes like fire, your voice like thunder, and told me I had no right to expect you to be a “proper daughter.” You left with your mother, and I have not seen either of you since. I have written to you, I have sent letters, and I have received none in return. You have not answered, nor have you replied to the few kind words I have sent, and I have come to believe that you would not have known how to answer, even if you had wished to.

I have tried to forget you, Eliza. I have tried to fill my days with the work of this lighthouse, with the care of the light, with the solitude that comes with such a life. I have not spoken of you,

**April 12th, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still wet from my quill, and the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky has turned the color of a man's soul—black, and heavy with the weight of things unsaid.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left me. Five years since I last heard your voice, and since I last saw your face. I have not spoken to you, nor have I written, for fear that my words might be too much for you to bear. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the need to speak. Not for myself, but for the lighthouse, for the men who have died under my watch, for the duty I have sworn to uphold, and for the son of your father—your father—who stands alone, as you once did, on the edge of the world.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the day you left. I remember the look in your eyes when you said, *“You don’t understand what I had to do.”* I remember the way you turned your back to me, and how I did not follow. I remember the silence that followed, and the silence that has remained.

I have kept the lighthouse, as I have kept my duty. I have kept my honor. I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the pain I have carried for years. Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I sit here by the light, the flame flickering in the darkness like a dying ember of hope. I do not know if I shall see dawn, and if I do, I do not know if I shall see you again.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me. I have not replied. I have not written. The silence between us has grown like the cliffs that guard this lighthouse—unyielding, unbroken, and yet, in the quiet, it aches.

You were once a child who would run to my side, your laughter like the crash of waves against the shore. You would ask me to tell you stories of the sea, of stars, of dreams that lay beyond the horizon. I would hold you close, whispering promises that the world would not be cruel, that love would endure. But the world is cruel, and love, I fear, has not endured.

I have not seen you in years, and I do not know if you still remember who I am. Perhaps you have forgotten. Perhaps you have forgiven me. Perhaps you have moved on. I do not know. But I write this now, not to beg for your return, but to say what I have always wanted to say.
